questions.description: "The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and…
questions.text: "The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and distinct from that of its members remains the general rule of law in Ireland to which there are a number of exceptions." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "When a [liquidator]{.underline} brings an application pursuant to the Companies Act to have the director of an…
questions.text: "When a [liquidator]{.underline} brings an application pursuant to the Companies Act to have the director of an insolvent company restricted, the director's only hope of not being restricted is if he can show that the director acted honestly and responsibly". You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company") and holds 10%… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company") and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given his 10% of the shares in the Company by his two fellow shareholding directors as a reward for his many years of service with the Company. The Company is in the printing business and Charlie is the chief typesetter who looks after the printing. Charlie's fellow directors, Richard and Martin each hold 45% of the issued shares in the Company. At the prompting of his brother-in-law, in whom Charlie has been confiding, Charlie comes to you for advice after learning that his fellow directors have recently transferred a printing press out of the Company to a friend of theirs in return for €50,000, which seems to be its market value. · Charlie tells you that the Company never really had formal board meetings and that things were no different on this occasion, Richard and Martin simply taking it upon themselves to give effect to the transfer. Charlie has explained that any time he has objected to this course of action, Richard and Martin have reminded him that once they agree to a course of action, there is little point in having a board meeting since the board acts by majority and he would be outvoted. They also remind him that as they hold 90% of the shares they can remove him as a director if they see fit so he has little reason to complain. You are required to advise Charlie on the law relating to minority shareholder oppression and the remedies available to a member of a company, and say whether in your opinion Charlie has a case against the Company or its directors. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Paul is the director of City Cars Limited (the "Company") which has… Fiduciary, Company.
questions.text: Paul is the director of City Cars Limited (the "Company") which has operated a business in North County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed by Simon although Paul has worked in the Company for 1O years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a company director with responsibility for sales five years ago. Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been "double-jobbing" for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Ludmila. Simon heard from a friend that Paul has been buying cars that were traded in to the Company at a discounted price and selling them on for a significant profit on his website. He also used the Company's workshop to service them before re-sale, again availing of discounts on .the time and materials charged. You are required to advise Simon on the law relating to directors' fiduciary duties and on the [case]{.underline} against Paul. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts presented above.

questions.description: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either three ways:…
questions.text: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly by an estoppel created with their unanimous informal agreement." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant cases and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, an official liquidator who… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, an official liquidator who was appointed to the Plot Gets Thicker Limited (the "Company"), which had been a sub-contractor in .the film production business. Gilbert was appointed liquidator on foot of a petition to have the Company wound up for failure to pay its debts which had been brought by the Revenue Commission. It appears that the Company is hopelessly insolvent having debts in excess of € 15,000,00 of which some €8,000,00 is owed to the Revenue Commissioners. Following his appointment, Gilbert began to investigate the Company's affairs and the behaviour of the Company's sole-shareholder and managing director, Reynard Fox ("Mr Fox"). Gilbert tells you: (a) The Company's books and records are in a sorry state. Record keeping has been at best sporadic, and Gilbert is finding it difficult to piece together the Company's financial history. It appears the film extras were often paid in cash and kept off the books of the Company as there are no records of people having being paid. However, other documents suggest that the Company had supplied hundreds of extras in a recent film in which it had been involved. Basic invoice keeping also appears to have been ignored and there is little or no financial documentation to support the claims which a number of creditors have lodged against the company for both goods and services allegedly supplied. (b) The Company appears to have either gifted or at best made a loan of some €150,000 to Mr Fox which he seems to have used to purchase a Bentley car in his own name. (c) The Company has also incurred massive debts with other film production companies. Its modus operandi seems to have been to agree to secure the acting services of big-name stars for up-coming films in return for the production companies paying the Company a significant fee of between €500,000 and €1,000,000. Gilbert has discovered that the Company was a party of no fewer than 6 such agreements with 6 different production companies and while it received some €5,000,000 from this business, it never once managed to procure the star for the film. The 6 creditors in question have complained to Gilbert that the Company and Mr Fox traded fraudulently or, at best, .recklessly in carrying on this business. Gilbert has found no evidence that the Company even sought the services of the stars in question, after taking the creditors' money. You are required to advise Gilbert on the grounds for applying to the High Court to have Reynard Fox made personally liable for some or all of the Company's debts. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of ORK Limited… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of ORK Limited (the "Company"), the other director of which is his wife, Deirdre. The Company operated the business of travel agent for a number of years. The Company has loan facilities from Big Bank pie which are secured by a floating charge over the Company's assets, a fixed charge over its leasehold interest in the premise it rents and the personal guarantees of its directors, Damien and Deirdre. The last six months were the worst ever experienced by the Company which made nearly all of its employees redundant. Wages were owed to some of the employees and one of them, Sean, was constantly calling to the family ·home of Damien and Deirdre ·seeking payment. In the last few weeks, the Revenue Commissioners petitioned to have the company wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts and the High Court granted that petition, appointing Jack Harmer the official liquidator. You are retained by Jack to advise as to the law relating of the following payments by the Company: (a) Christmas week, when Sean called at the directors' home seeking payment, Damien was out and Deirdre, who was concerned at Sean's ever-threatening manner, wrote him a cheque for €5,000 in respect of wages owed to him; (b) The morning that the petition was heard by the High Court, Damien transferred €30,000 from one account the Company had into its overdrawn current account ; (c) The day after the petition was presented, the Company's bank debited the Company's current account for €10,000 in respect of accrued interest in the Company's loans. (d) A month after the petition to wind up the Company, the liquidator himself made a payment from the Company 's overdrawn account to the Company 's landlord in respect of the premises it occupied as otherwise its lease would have been forfeited . You are required to advise Jack the liquidator on the law in relation to each of the four payments, distinguishing (a), (b), (c) and (d) in your answer. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The fact that certain types of retention of title clauses must be registered in the Companies Registration Office in…
questions.text: "The fact that certain types of retention of title clauses must be registered in the Companies Registration Office in order to be valid whereas certain other types of such clauses need not be r registered illustrates that not all security interests created by companies are required to be registered under the Companies Act in order to be valid." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer. March 2015

questions.description: "The general rule that fiduciary duties are owed by company directors to the company (and to the company alone) is so…
questions.text: "The general rule that fiduciary duties are owed by company directors to the company (and to the company alone) is so well established and acknowledged that it barely admits any exception". Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "company"),…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "company"), established by their late mother Madge, and acquired by them under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and it's only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not has any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend with the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they'd be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah have come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town where the Company had its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked to register as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's articles of association, you see the following provision: "the directors may, in their absolute discretion, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register any transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share".

questions.description: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in lunch Time… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in lunch Time Treats Limited (The "Company") which is in the business of supplying fresh pre-packed sandwiches, rolls, salads and other foodstuffs to local shops which do not have the facilities for making-up their own produce. The Company's business is holding up reasonably well in the recession but their greatest headache is in getting paid for goods supplied. The directors decide that they are going to take a harder line with the shops to which they supply food. It is decided that where payment of a sum in excess of €15,000 is overdue for 6 months, the Company will issue such shops (that are themselves limited companies) with a 21-day letter demanding payment and in the event of non-compliance, the Company will present a petition to have the debtor company would up on the grounds of being unable to pay its debts. You are required to advise Richard and Judy on the law relating to the winding up of companies on the grounds of being unable to pay its debts. You are required to identify possible issues that might arise in the context of the facts provided. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: 'A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations.' You are required to discuss this…
questions.text: 'A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations.' You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: 'The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and…
questions.text: 'The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and distinct from that of its members remains the general rule of law in Ireland to which there are a number of exceptions". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and stature law in your answer.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the 'Bank') to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the 'Company') on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for €2,500,000 in 2007. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1,500,000 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Retail Motors Limited (the "Company") is in dire financial straits. Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Retail Motors Limited (the "Company") is in dire financial straits. For some months now, its shareholders and directors, John and Timothy, have realised that the Company's used-car business is hopelessly insolvent. The Company owes €750,000 to the Revenue Commissioners in VAT, PAYE and PRSI; it owes €200,000 on its bank overdraft and also owes in the aggregate €500,000 to other creditors, comprised of suppliers and customers. The Company's assets are €275,000, comprised of cars worth approximately €250,000 and about €25,000 in cash. John and Timothy have been dodging their creditors all summer but two of them have been particularly tenacious, one a customer and the other a trade creditor. The Customer (Fred) is owed €15,000 for a car he sold the Company. Fred, who is an old school friend of Timothy, has been telephoning him and calling around to his house on a nightly basis seeking payment. Eventually Fred caught up with Timothy and after a heated conversation when Fred actually pushed Timothy against a wall and warned him of the consequences of not paying him the money, Timothy capitulated and paid Fred the €15,000 owed to him. On being told by Timothy of what had happened, John decided that they must pay the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation and initiated this the following day when a resolution was passed to place the company into creditors' liquidation. The trade creditor was a firm that has previously carried out repair work to used cars for the Company. This firm was owed €40,000 and its Managing Director (Mike) had become increasingly abusive to John and Timothy, and most recently to Timothy's wife June whom he badly frightened after he made a telephone call to her home. In consequence of this, the day after the resolution was passed to put the Company into creditors' voluntary winding up, Timothy gave a cheque to Mike, drawn from the Company's already overdrawn bank account. You are required to advise the Company's liquidator on the validity of the two payments made by the Company: (i) the first for €15,000 to Fred and (ii) the second for €40,000 to Mike's firm. Your answer must clearly distinguish between (i) and (ii) and you must cite relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: The liquidator of Joe's Joinery Limited (The "Company") contacts you for… Company, Director.
questions.text: The liquidator of Joe's Joinery Limited (The "Company") contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has just been appointed to the Company by the high court on the petition of the Revenue Commissioners and needs your help in unravelling the Company's affairs and determining the order of priorities. Specifically, you are advised that: (a) A company called Raw Materials Limited has a retention of title clause in its contract for the sale of three batches of timber it supplied to the Company. The Liquidator advises you that there are only two batches untouched as the third was made into furniture. The clause in question provided "The property of the timber shall not transfer to the Company until all sums owing to the Vendor have been paid and until then any goods made from the timber shall be the property of the Vendor". The Liquidator wants to know whether he must return the timber and the furniture made therefrom. (b) Another supplier claims to have a fixed charge over one of the Company's bank accounts. On further enquiry the Liquidator was shown a copy of a deed of fixed charge over the Company's book debts which required them to be paid into a designated bank account. As far as the Liquidator can see the account was operated by the Company whose directors lodged and withdrew monies therefrom at will. The liquidator wants to know whether this charge gives the supplier priority over the Revenue Commissioners. (c) The Company created a fixed charge over a site of land in favour of Small Bank plc to secure an overdraft it had with the Bank. It transpires that the charge which was only created 15 days' ago, does not appears to have been registered in the Companies Registration Office. The liquidator wants to know whether this charge can still be registered and the consequences for Small Bank plc if it is not registered. You are required to advise the Liquidator in respect of each of the foregoing matters. Your answer should clearly distinguish the three cases outlined in (a), (b) and (c) and you must cite all relevant cases and stature law.
